Many corruptions hide from even the most careful leaders. Some people disobeyed God’s clear command that forbade marriage with non-believers. Disbelief in God's ability to provide is at the root of our attempts to help ourselves. They exposed themselves and their children to the danger of idolatry, which had ruined their church and nation. People who outwardly profess faith may dismiss these connections and try to explain away instructions to be separate; but those who know God’s word best will treat this subject differently. They must expect the worst from such unions. The excuses and justifications used by many professors are shocking and cause regret in true believers. All who claim to be God's people should support those who oppose vice and ungodliness.

The sacrifice, especially the evening sacrifice, was a symbol of the blessed Lamb of God, who at the end of the world would take away sin through his own sacrifice. Ezra’s speech is a sorrowful confession of sin, the sin of his people. But let this be a comfort to true penitents: though their sins reach to the heavens, God's mercy is even higher. Ezra speaks as if deeply ashamed. Holy shame is as necessary in true repentance as holy sorrow. Ezra speaks as if greatly amazed. Discoveries of guilt cause amazement; the more we think about sin, the worse it appears. Say, “God be merciful to me, a sinner.” Ezra speaks as if very afraid. There is no surer or sadder sign of ruin than returning to sin after great judgments and deliverances. Everyone in God’s church should wonder that they have not exhausted the Lord's patience and brought destruction upon themselves. What then must be the situation of those who are ungodly? But though a true penitent has nothing to say in his own defense, the heavenly Advocate pleads powerfully on his behalf.
